agent_raw: The payments company's fraud model comparison cannot support the migration decision. The benchmark is broken by a label maturation mismatch: the baseline (gradient boosted tree) was evaluated against 30-day provisional fraud labels from Q1, while the challenger (larger ensemble) was evaluated against 90-day matured labels from Q2. These are different fraud target definitions — 90-day labels capture additional chargebacks and resolved disputes that have not yet appeared at 30 days. A richer label set mechanically increases PR-AUC when the model captures those additional cases. The six-basis-point gain may therefore reflect label completeness, not model quality. Compounding this, the evaluation windows differ (Q1 vs. Q2), and a merchant onboarding campaign changed the Q2 transaction mix. This population shift adds a second independent confound. Finally, a 0.006 PR-AUC difference at 0.2% fraud prevalence is a small and potentially noisy estimate that requires a paired bootstrap confidence interval to be interpretable. The temporal split by week is the correct approach for fraud and is not at issue. The required correction is a common-quarter evaluation with matched 90-day labels and a paired bootstrap confidence interval for the PR-AUC difference.
all_issues_raised: Label maturation mismatch: 30-day baseline vs. 90-day challenger
Different evaluation quarters with merchant onboarding population shift
No confidence interval for small gain at rare fraud prevalence
